tokens: Now, we don't have anything left but a little rendezvous.
(Sounding like someone about to enter a haunted house) Ha, ha, ha, ha.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Give me your purge valve thing, I'll put it in the TSB (Temporary Storage Bag).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Wait a minute; let me give you my purge valve.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Finishing the thought)...got all that dirt on it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Garbled) purge valve because it has to rotate with the other...Got your...
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: "Purge valve and OPS O2 hose; stow purge valves in TSB (Temporary Stowage Bag."
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: All right, just threw it in there, I'll find the ball.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Oh, may have thrown it over.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: These things (the OPS oxygen hose or his gloves) won't come undone!
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Bet they've got a lot of that dust on them.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Red to red; blue to blue.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: So far, we haven't touched one of those circuit breakers as far as I can tell.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Long Pause) "Red to red; blue to blue; (suit) isolation (valves) both to Suit Flow."
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, would you turn mine on?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Does that (flow of LM oxygen) feel good!
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let's take the "PLSS (LCG water) pump, Off; and (PLSS) fan Off"; it (the LM O2)'s backpressuring in your fan.
Connect LM H2O." I'll get yours and you get mine.
(Pause) Lift up your RCU a little bit, Pete (so that he can see the connectors better).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: "(LM) LCG Pump (circuit breaker), In (that is, Closed)."
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're going to get cooler, probably.
"PLSS mode, both of them O, (that is) Off."
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, turn your VHF A, Off, and B, Off.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: A, Off, and B is Off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, Intrepid hooked up on the Intrepid system.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: They never told us to turn our tape recorder on.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We read you loud and clear, Pete, and we're standing by to give you any help on the stowage you may need.
And, when you get down to it, we also have some good words for you on how to stow the (LM) TV camera.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, we'll wait for a while.
Let us get through our checklist, please.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Are we a little bit early, or do you want me to go ahead with the checklist and put on my rendezvous radar operate heater circuit breaker (in order to warm up the electronics)?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause) Pete, we show that you are ahead.
You can go ahead and complete that step, and we'll be back with you on any items which we would like you to hold on (that is, delay execution).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'll go ahead and put that in, and we're going to go ahead with our jettison here shortly.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's right, and we'll have the LMP's for you in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, as soon as we get through our suit integrity check, we will be depressing the cabin for the jettison.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We are just cleaning up the cockpit.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We copy two impacts on the PSE during jettison.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: As a matter of fact, that may help you calibrate that thing, because I got a clean kick on both of them.
In other words, when they left the hatch, they departed the hatch and free-fell all the way to the ground without touching the ladder or anything on the way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: They pretty much went straight out...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...on a ballistic right on down and hit the ground.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We're changing over sites in about 10 seconds (means minutes).
We may be losing comm for a minute or two.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We have some words for you on the stowage of the TV and also the equipment which is put on the floor by the (plus) Z-27.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We're right at that point (at the bottom of the left-hand column of
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And, first, you could fold the TV handle and stow it (the TV) in the LiOH canister bracket on the (back of the) engine cover, lens up.
Secure the TV cable end to the camera, pad the camera with utility towels (which are stowed) in the upper boot bay.
Replace the knurled knob hold-down, secure the camera with the utility straps.
Wrap the straps around the lens and the knurled knobs.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Intrepid, give me a call when you are ready for some information on the equipment to go by Z-27.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: You can expect lost Comm here for about 1 to 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Start talking to us about the gear you want us to tie down back of the OPSs.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Okay, first point there is to ensure that the
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Say the last part again, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We suggest that you use two additional strands of the utility straps.
Now, this would go from the top of the jettison bag to the ISA fitting; that's the D ring on the midsection step.
This will give you a total of four strands.

(Pause) Essentially what we're looking for here, Al, is to tie this thing down with as many strands as you can - at least four - so that in the docking we don't get this thing coming off the floor and busting the straps.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Pete and Al are just finishing up the post-EVA, and it looks as though they're pretty far ahead.
They have a little bit of time to sit back and relax.
We have a map update for Rev 29 when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Tell those guys to sit back and relax.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Tell those guys they deserve a rest.
They can sit back and relax.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Ed, it sounds like they didn't have any problems down there at all.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: They ran into a few things; but, as they did yesterday, they overcame them.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I expect most of the problems are human.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Maybe so, Dick, but not many of them theirs.
Dick, would you go back in your flight plan to 131:30?
And we missed a crew status report at that point.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Says here I had 7 hours sleep, PRD (garbled) (Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We still have a lot of garbling, and broken up.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We have everything stowed, secured properly, and we're ready to start the launch countdown at the proper time.
And, if you'll give us about 15 or 20 minutes to chow down here, we'll come back with you and have a little chitty chat about the EVA.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) And, Pete, you're still quite a bit ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It looks as though the furthest you could go up to in the checklist is on
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We're just sitting here now; we've got the spacecraft all squared away.
I'll say everything's tied down, but man, oh man, is it filthy in here; we must have 20 pounds of dust, dirt, and all kinds of junk.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That'll be an interesting (return to) zero g.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Al and I look just like a couple of bituminous coal miners right at the moment.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: So are a lot of people down here.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Say, let me get a time hack with you on my mission timer.
I'm reading 137:40:53, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 137:41; is that about right?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're a couple of seconds off; we can give you a more accurate time hack if you like.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'll bring the computer out of Standby and get the time okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I just happened to think about it; I haven't messed with the mission timer since we landed.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Houston, how's the SIDE doing?
Did that cold gas jobber-do get running or not?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by on that, Pete; we'll get the latest word on it.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Also, how are the package temperatures doing?
Are they running like they expected?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, looks good on both counts.
The CCIG came up and is looking nominal, and the temperatures are looking nominal.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And I'll tell you, from watching those plots from down here, that PSE is sure doing the job.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: If you'll look at our schedule there, we've been running our tape recorder according to the schedule; and, because the EVAs ran longer, do we have anything left on that for ascent and rendezvous?
Can you check that out for us?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'd like to give you the T17 and T18 lift-off Pads before CSM LOS, coming up in about 8 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Long Pause while they get the data book out) This is Intrepid ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Yankee Clipper, are you ready to copy?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And, Houston, Intrepid standing by to copy the P22 (radar tracking of the Command Module) Acq(uisition) time and the new octal numbers and we're also ready to debrief with you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're not planning to do that P22.
The job's been done already, so we can forget the P22 coming up at 140 plus 00.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And, Intrepid, we have your consumables Pad when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Consumables at GET (Ground Elapsed Time) of 137:00: RCS A, 80 percent; B, 76; O2, and we'll give you first the descent and the ascent.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And on your question on the (Data Storage Electronics Assembly, DSEA) tape recorder.
We show that, under the way that we'd normally figure it, that by extending those two EVAs, we come up with 10-1/2 hours, whereas the tape recorder normally goes 10.

However, those assumptions are: first, 100-percent activity (that is, the recorder is on all the time) in the descent, ascent, and the rendezvous and 30-percent activity (that is, the recorder is on 30 percent of the time) on the EVA.

The 100-percent figure is most likely high and the 30-percent figure is most likely low; so it's pretty difficult for us to come up and tell you exactly when you're going to be cutting off in the ascent or if you will at all.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll just go ahead and run it per the schedule.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And our first comment on the EVA.
The Gold Flight Team members back here would like to give you their congratulations for a job well done.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And, Intrepid, we have a few questions.
First one of which is on the weigh bags and their cracking.
Can you briefly describe the problem that you encountered in cracking and what the reason may be for it?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, they seem to...When I folded the first big one...Let me think a minute.
The contingency sample one (that is, the contingency sample bag), I believe, has some holes in it, doesn't it, Al?
(Pause) I can't remember on that but, anyhow, the first big one (weigh bag) wound up (with) just about an inch-and-a-half-long crack (that) appeared in it when I was folding it.
Just like a fatigue failure; and that appears to be pretty much what happened.
I noticed a couple of the saddlebags were the same way after we got done rattling around with them.
They had some 1- or 2-inch-long cracks which made holes in them.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Can you relate it to a given thermal situation?
In other words, were they all cold at the time or really couldn't you say that?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I think you all realize - from the angle that we're at (that is, the LM orientation on the surface)- that the MESA was in the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: At least, I wasn't aware of that one.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It's not completely in the Sun, but it's almost in the Sun.
When I say "almost", you know there's some gear struts throwing some shadows and everything, but that area was in sunlight.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: See you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Could you discuss the failure of the camel hander (sic)...(correcting himself) the camera handle?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The knurled knob came off the shaft that it was mounted to, therefore allowing all the pieces to fall apart.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And how did the hammer fail?
Did it fail in some way other than just the chips of the coating coming off?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Just the coating was coming off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What problems did you have with the S-band in terms of (tripping on) the cable; and, also, exactly where did you put it?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, just the fact that I wasn't looking where I was going.
I'd get tangled up in the S-band antenna wire every once in a while.
That one's not as bad as the TV (cable), though, because the TV one doesn't lay flat; the S-band one lays flat.
We put the S-band out just like we agreed to do it - to minimized running over the wire - which was out the
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And, Pete, when you went back to check the CCIG, could you tell us how close you got to it?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I walked up to within about 5 feet of it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Okay, and a question on the third film pack which we used.
How much of that was used on the inside, and where in the traverse did you pick it up and change it to one of the existing cameras?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, I've got some bad news for you and some good news.
In the first place, the third magazine was a color magazine, and all it had on it were some shots that were taken of Earthrise and a few things like that coming around on descent.
And, unfortunately, Al and I got our signals crossed, and it's outside on the lunar surface right now.

Now, what we did was take the black-and-white magazine off of Al's camera when it failed and put it on my camera and used it up so that we have two complete black and whites of the second EVA and two complete colors of the first EVA, and the only thing that's missing is the color magazine that had undocking and a couple other mundane things like that on it at the beginning of the LM operation.
And, unfortunately, that's out there in that saddle bag.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You did get the Surveyor, though?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We have all the Surveyor pictures and everything, but they're all black and white.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And lastly, we've been looking at your power consumption profile and notice that you're not using as much as they initially anticipated.
They attribute that to the lack of use of the floodlights.
Would you confirm that you've not had the floodlights on since the beginning of the first EVA?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We haven't had the floodlights on.
And, also, we've used LCG pump very, very little.
Every once in a while we give ourselves a squirt.
The spacecraft, in contrast to Neil's...We've been very warm.
And we've been comfortably warm with just the air in our suits, and I'd say the temperature inside here right now is running in the low 70s somewhere.
And it's been that way ever since we got here.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's the last of our questions.
We could finish this up if you give us two PRD readings.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Long Pause) Al is 04022, and I'm still 11020.
It'd kind of give you the idea mine may have quit running.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We copy a 04022, and say the second (again).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: 11020, and I think that's what it was the last time I gave it to you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: A question on the equipment jettison.
Was there anything other than what's already called out for that was jettisoned or not jettisoned?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, we jettisoned everything according to the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Pete, how does the inside of the cabin look about now?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Except for...(Stops to listen to Gibson) It's very neat and orderly except for the fact that it's very dirty.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Kind of a neat, orderly coal mine.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That's about the size of it.
The only thing that we do have (that was not jettisoned as per the checklist), of course, is the LiOH canister container which now has the TV camera in it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we also understood from Al out there on the surface that only partially deployed; only got up around 60 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I was the one that put it up; and, when I put it back down, I discovered that I hadn't turned it the last, about, 20 percent.
And, so, it went all the way up, and then I stowed it.
I didn't put it all the way up.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We had probably the best of any Sim we've had.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Yeah, I concur; the comm really has been super.
(Pause) These PLSSs and suits performed magnificently.
These suits are a shambles (that is, dirty) though.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Pete and Al, when you climbed back in, you terminated at 3 plus 55.
And, (for) both of you, your reserve was determined by oxygen.
In PLSS 1 (Pete's) the reserve was 2 plus 05, which would have given you a 6-hour PLSS; and the PLSS 2 was 1 plus 50, which is pretty close to 6 hours also.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That's very good to know, and there's no reason why you can't stay out there and work that long.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Get a little thirsty, though, I bet.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We were really thirsty after the second EVA because...I don't know, did you add up how far we went?
I think we made a pretty good trip out there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're estimating something over a mile for the full circuit.
But that's not counting some of the side jaunts you made.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We've been trying to follow our tracks (of footprints) out here with the monocular.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The other thing that I...Have you got your map book there?
Let me talk to you about this big blocky-rimmed crater that's out here.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: On the great big map, it's the crater that has got the really big blocks on it, that's just outside the ellipse on map A.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Which is the great big map?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The smaller one (that is, the 1:5,000 map) is...(Answering Gibson) Okay, the one that shows the landing ellipse.
Let me look just a second.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Maybe the use of that LM shadow then was pretty useful.
I know in the beginning (at 112:07:13), you doubted that the shadow was really that long, and apparently it was telling you the truth.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The other thing is (that), from the spacecraft here looking at the ALSEP, it looks like it's right under the window.
And Al and I's best guess is that it's at least 450 feet away now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, I got a question for you, Houston.
What do the experts think about doing an alignment (of the inertial platform) in orbit now, versus what we got here on the lunar surface yesterday?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Also, that crater which you were talking about is, we estimate, 4-1/2 kilometers from your present position.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Looks like it's just a hop, skip, and a jump.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Responding to Pete's question about doing an alignment of the inertial platform) Pete, we'd like to go ahead and stick with the procedures we have right now.
We can give you an update on it after the two P57's after at insertion.
There's a chance that we may not need it.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, I wasn't proposing that we didn't do it; we're going to go ahead and do it.
I was just curious, after we got a good RLS - which we've never gotten before - out of the P57 yesterday, what everybody thought about that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Houston, one thing that occurred to me...The reason we're not using the floodlights is because that overhead hatch is not rigged right and that microswitch is open all the time - I should say the microswitch isn't rigged right; the hatch is okay.
And those floodlights were on all the time, so when we were getting ready to come down over the night side, we had the floods on, just the two lower ones were still always on.
So, we pulled that breaker, and we've left it out all the rest of the time.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Pete and Al, do you think that if you were out there for say 4-1/2, maybe even 5 hours, you'd get hungry as well as thirsty on an extended traverse?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I don't think so, if you chowed down good just before you left.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Maybe they can come up with something that you can put on the inside of your helmet, something like - not exactly like - the little valsalva device.
Just something where you could maybe reach over and take even a swallow of water.
You're only thirsty because your throat is dry, and if you just had something - one swallow - I think that'd fix you up right there.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Say, did either one of you kneel down in order to get anything off the surface, or did you use the newly-developed Bean technique of holding on to the Surveyor parts bag and lowering the Commander to the surface?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, we used all kinds of things like that.
You could take the shovel and stick it in the ground and just do a one-arm pushup and lean down and pick up a rock off the ground with the other hand.
It's really a ridiculous way to do it.
If you had a suit that would bend, why, you'd have the whole program wired.
I fell over once out there, and Al picked me back up again.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: But, in the same sense, you're always fussing around trying to get down there to get these rocks, and we did kneel down a couple of times.
I knelt down and picked some stuff up.
And it's particularly easy if you got that Hand Tool Carrier with you.
But we really do need to come up with some sort of strap or something that would allow you to lean over and grab a rock that won't fit in those tongs.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (We're) picking up the Launch Prep Verb 98 at LO (Lift-Off) minus 02:50.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's the same thing we had before, also.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We have a update on the star Arcturus on
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And we passed self-test all right.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Are you ready for E-MOD dump?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: If you will give us P00 and Accept, we'll give you a CSM state vector and RLS update.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I hope you would like to have some company for a change.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: As a matter of fact, I just finished that.
I sure do; got everything in order; ready to go towards the LM and bring back (garbled).
That's quite a chore; keeping this thing clean.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You got a couple of coal miners coming up to see you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I'll be glad to see them.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Yankee Clipper, if you will go P00 and ACCEPT, we have an uplink.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Houston, (have) you got the lift-off time for me?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And Gerry, will you find out what they want to do about this battery charge, because I'm using the bus ties during the rendezvous?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Why don't you figure on terminating the battery charge at LOS?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: All right; I could let it go until I (garbled) just before lift-off.
That way it might take it all the way up.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We prefer that you terminate at LOS on this pass.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That would be one less thing for us to keep track of prior to lift-off.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Long Pause) Yankee Clipper, Houston with a P22 tracking Pad.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: T1 is 139:57:39; T2 is 140:02:38; south 05; latitude is minus 3...
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Latitude, minus 3.036; longitude over 2 is minus 11.709; altitude minus 1.13.
If you want to take photos, your DAC settings are 1/60th, 1 over 60, one SP ...
You should be at zero local horizontal rather than 22 degrees pitchdown.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: T1, 139:57:39, T2, 140:02:38, 5 miles south, latitude is minus 3.036, longitude over 2 is minus 11.709; altitude minus 1.13 and (garbled) information.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: And I've got a REV 30 map update when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And, Dick, I've got your consumables update when you are ready.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: At a GET of 139 plus 20, RCS total was 56 percent; Alpha is 58; Bravo, 56; Charlie, 56; Delta, 55; hydrogen tank 1 is 49.9; tank 2 is 49.8; oxygen, 52.7 and 54.4.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And my rendezvous radar transponder self-check is okay.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Hey, Gerry, I would like to have a DAP update also, please.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Looking at the DSKY torquing angles?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: How's your drinking water intake been since you got back in?
You been replacing quite a bit of it?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: If you'll give us P00 and ACCEPT, we have another LM state vector for you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I'm going to stop my roll (garbled) here.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Is there any objection if we track him when he goes by this time?
And do you have the OSCIL, or don't you want us to do it?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We hadn't really planned on doing a P22 on this pass.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: About the only change you need is you need to go to 0.5-degree deadband, so R1 should read 11101.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Are the weights and trim verified yet?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Your weights and your trims are good.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That's what I was worried about.
I'll probably go to a half a degree a second during the rendezvous because those maneuvers are pretty long.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: On your maneuver, go S-band OMNI Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Give me an overhead time, so I can watch him go by.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) I'm going to check the plane change and see how good you are.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Clipper wants to know if you're up VHF.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: No, but we will come up VHF.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Ask him whether he wants us up VHF A or B SIMPLEX.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What would you like, VHF A or B SIMPLEX?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: How about the normal VHF ranging (garbled)?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Come up on VHF ranging configuration.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Are you going to use the zero-degree pitch or 22-degree pitch down for this pass?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Hello, Yankee Clipper; Intrepid on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, I don't like those marks at all, that Sun angle was pretty high.
That whole area is washed out; and the best that I could tell you (is that) I think I was on the Surveyor Crater but I can't be sure of that.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That Sun's a little too high to define that right now.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We had a visual on him although I couldn't talk to him on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Did you read Intrepid on VHF?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You sure it wasn't S-band because, you know, we're in a relay mode.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Pause) I think I read VHF, Gerry.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Hey, Gerry, I sure wouldn't do anything with that P22 data because I'm just not that sure of it.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We'll give that data a good evaluation before we do anything with it.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Got sort of an interesting thing going on the
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: If I turn down the illumination level just a little bit, it's not noticeable.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You ready for my RCS hot fire?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: He and I have both seen that phenomena on your DEDA during testing of most all the spacecrafts (sic) up at (the Grumman plant at) Bethpage, (New York), and it's probably an EMI.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That's what we've been talking about, but we thought we'd just touch in on it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I think TRW's got a workup on this problem.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Here you go, Houston, with roll, pitch, and yaw (tests of the RCS).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We just blew over our S-band erectable (with the exhaust from the RCS), and we're up on our steerable.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I was just going to tell you, Pete, we lost some of the data on that fire check.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You want me to give it to you again?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I'll tell you what we need.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I never gave you yaw anyhow.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, can you just start over from the beginning?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Pitch up is the only one that didn't sound right.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've got a K-factor update for you when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: R1 is 00140; R2 is all zips (that is, all zeroes); R3 is 00033.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Give us a chance to take a look at our tapes.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The passive seismometer just verified that you did do your hot fire.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Long Pause) (Amused) Does the passive seismometer say my hot fire's Go (that is, that it was satisfactory)?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, are you ready for our gyro torquing angles (via downlink from the CSM computer)?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I have a LM ascent Pad and a CSI PAD (as per the top of the right-hand column of
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Are you ready for the rest of my hot fire?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Clipper, you can go ahead and torque.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I gave you an extra pitchup fire because we were photographing the effects on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we're ready to copy the Launch Pad.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: LM ascent Pad follows: Tig 142:03:47.00; Noun 76, 5535.0 (this will be their horizontal velocity at engine shutdown), 0037.0, plus 000.2; DEDA 47 is plus 37364, plus 05607, plus 58642, plus 56955; DEDA 465 is plus 00370; DEDA 546 is NA (not applicable); ignition one Rev late is 144:02:09; LM weight, 10789; CSM weight, 35390.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Noun 11, 143:01:50.60; Noun 37, 144:38, all zips; Noun 81, 0492, all zips; DEDA 373 is 01818; 275 is 02780; AGS Delta-V's, plus 0492, all zips, plus 0010.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I'm standing by for your up-data link of the LGC (LM Guidance Computer) gyro compensation.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You can terminate your battery Bravo charge now.
And we'd like to have you put your O2 tank 1 heaters and your hydrogen tank 2 heaters back to Auto.
And dump your waste water to between...
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I have got the H2 and O2 heaters, and dump the water to 52 percent.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: And I'll do a purge next.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I'll do a purge on the oxygen and hydrogen fuel cell.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Pete, we'll be sending your gyro compensation uplink to you after you've done your second P57 (which starts at the bottom of
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay, must be a mistake in the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's poorly placed in the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: How'd the hot fire look all the way around?
(Long Pause) Got another question for you, too, Houston.
What did Yankee Clipper's orbit finally decay down to?
How well did you hit 60 (nautical miles)?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's 61.9 by 58.4 at CDH (Constant Delta Height).
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Yeah, that's pretty good, and we're targeted for zero CDH here on this thing, right?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're looking good, and we're looking for an AOS at 141:17.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Any objections to us starting P57 now?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We prefer that you wait until (launch minus) four-five (minutes, as per
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You mean (Ground Elapsed Time 141:)15, I hope.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) We were thinking in terms of lift-off minus 45 (minutes).
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Would you put battery 5 on the line now?
Before, it wasn't really carrying its load as well as it should have, and we'd kind of like to pre-precondition that one, get it a little warmer and get it started early.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That's exactly what it did prior to descent: not much.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Clipper, I read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Your state vectors are all good, so there'll be no uplink to you this time.
Your map update Pad is scratched, and I've got a
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Do you want us to do this one, or can we skip it?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: The reason I say that, Gerry; since I can't see them at these high Sun angles, it's pretty academic to do this one.
(Pause) I'd just as soon save the gas (it would take to position the spacecraft) and skip it.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: The data is only in the event you want to try to watch lift-off.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I don't think I can see them, Gerry, so let's skip it.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Your gravity angle difference looks good and, there'll be no uplinks to you this time.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: About the time when you get VHF communications established with Intrepid, we're going to dump the MSFN relay.

If for some reason you lose it and you want to hear the Intrepid lift-off, let us know, and we can reconfigure in about 20 seconds; but we would prefer to leave the relay out as long as you've got VHF.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Long Pause) Boy, this place is fascinating!
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Pretty nice looking torquing angles, Intrepid.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You're go to cast off on this Rev.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Houston, did you copy the values of 047 and 053?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That's affirmative, Al, and they're Go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Houston having looked over the tank pressure telemetry) Intrepid, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Are you recommending VHF A Receiver On or Off for launch?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Need to have your VHF A Transmitter to Voice Range; and Receiver, Off.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Hello, Houston; Intrepid is on VOX.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Checklist is complete; standing by for Tig minus 2.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: On my time, Yankee Clipper, it will be 1 minute.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: First stage (means 'abort stage') push at 30 seconds, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Pause) Watch the ALSEP, and I'll fly the bird.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: All we lack is Pro(ceed) and then, after, we get Engine Start.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Firing like I don't know what.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Thirty seconds; 177 (feet per second horizontal velocity), 984.6 (feet per second vertical velocity), and out at 1900 feet (altitude).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And at 1 minute, yaw right 20, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Kind of wobbles around up here.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Both tank pressures look good, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Sure jumps every time those thrusters fire.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It's just changing CG (Center of Gravity).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's right on the pitch profile.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Looks like some of the same territory we've passed over before, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Two minutes and 30 seconds, looking at 1373 (horizontal velocity), 187 (vertical velocity), climbing out at 19,700 (feet).
Houston, you better clear me out of flight level 240 for flight level 600.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: How does your high gain look, Al?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: High gain looks real good, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: If you can, have him transmit VHF also.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Dick would like you to transmit on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: 2130 feet per second, climbing 193, and we're out at 31,600.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Sequence) camera went off sometime after lift-off.
I hope it got the ALSEP (as they flew over).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Wonder why we got the Master Alarm?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Oh, look at that rille down there.
(I) think that was part of Lansberg, I think, down there on the left.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (The LM is) starting move around a little bit more now as we lighten up.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You've got a big job now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've been thinking about them since we lifted off.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Five minutes, 3403, 156 feet, and out at 47,000.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The harbor master has cleared you into the main channel.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Pause) Look at that lunar surface, would you.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Man, look at that crater we're flying over.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 1400 feet per second to go (in horizontal velocity).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, I'm going to get over on Verb 16, Noun 85 right now.
This thing's running a little bit hot.
transcript.speaker: Conrad
